name: Orion Correlation Theory
description: The Orion Correlation Theory (OCT) proposes a direct correlation between the location of the three largest pyramids of the Giza pyramid complex – Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure – and the three stars of Orion's Belt: Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka. First proposed by Robert Bauval in 1994, the theory suggests that the relative positions of the pyramids on the ground mirror the relative positions of these stars in the sky as they appeared in 10,500 BC, an epoch associated with the astrological Age of Leo. Bauval and others argue this alignment was intentional, reflecting the ancient Egyptians' deep reverence for Orion, which they associated with the god Osiris, and their belief in an afterlife journey mirroring Orion's celestial path. Proponents cite various pieces of evidence, including the alignment of air shafts within the Great Pyramid towards specific stars, and textual references linking Osiris with Orion. Mainstream Egyptologists generally reject the OCT, arguing that the proposed correlation is statistically insignificant and based on selective interpretation of evidence. They point out that the pyramids were built over several decades during the Old Kingdom (c. 2589–2504 BC) and that any apparent alignment with Orion is coincidental. Furthermore, the precise dating of the pyramids and their internal features is well-established through archaeological evidence, contradicting the 10,500 BC claim. Critics also highlight that the OCT requires significant adjustments to the positions of the pyramids and the stars to achieve the purported correlation, and that the Egyptians' understanding of astronomy was sophisticated but not necessarily focused on such precise celestial mirroring.
subtitle: Giza Pyramids and Orion's Belt alignment

claims.claim: The Giza pyramids' locations correlate with Orion's Belt stars.
claims.evidence: The relative positions of the three largest Giza pyramids mirror the relative positions of Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka.
claims.criticism: The correlation is statistically insignificant and requires selective interpretation of data. Minor shifts in pyramid location make the alignment more apparent.

claims.claim: This alignment was intentional, reflecting Egyptian reverence for Orion/Osiris.
claims.evidence: Ancient Egyptian texts link Osiris with Orion, suggesting a belief in an afterlife journey mirroring Orion's celestial path. Air shafts within the Great Pyramid point to specific stars.
claims.criticism: The interpretation of ancient texts is subjective. Air shaft alignment may have served other purposes, like ventilation, and the target stars are debated.

claims.claim: The alignment dates to 10,500 BC, marking a significant epoch for the Egyptians.
claims.evidence: The proposed alignment is most accurate for the sky as it appeared in 10,500 BC, aligning with the astrological Age of Leo.
claims.criticism: The pyramids were built during the Old Kingdom (c. 2589–2504 BC), centuries after 10,500 BC. There is no archaeological evidence to support construction at that date.

keyFigures.name: Robert Bauval
keyFigures.role: Primary proponent and author of 'The Orion Mystery'

keyFigures.name: Ed Krupp
keyFigures.role: Astronomer and critic, Director of Griffith Observatory, who challenges the OCT

keyFigures.name: I.E.S. Edwards
keyFigures.role: Egyptologist and critic of the Orion Correlation Theory

mythologyConnections: Re (Egyptian sun god, journey across the sky)

timeline.event: Robert Bauval publishes 'The Orion Mystery' introducing the theory to a wider audience.
Ongoing debate and discussion of the theory within both academic and alternative history communities.
The Orion Correlation Theory continues to be a popular topic in alternative history and conspiracy circles.

sources.author: Emily Teeter
sources.work: Religion and Ritual in Ancient Egypt
sources.title: Emily Teeter
